sections.heading: When to Use Functions
sections.body:
Functions can run arbitrary JavaScript code that you define, which means
you can use them for almost anything. Common use cases include
low-latency, short-running tasks like data movement, transformations,
and validation. You can also use them to connect to external services
and abstract away implementation details from your client applications.

[Atlas Triggers](atlas-triggers) automatically call Functions to handle specific
events. For example, whenever a Database Trigger observes a change event it
calls its associated Function with the change event as an argument. In the
Trigger Function, you can then access information from the change event and
respond appropriately.

> **Note:** Database and Scheduled Triggers always execute in the context of a
> system user.

sections.heading: How to Write a Function

codeBlocks.content:
Right now NEW YORK CITY is 72°F and sunny.
Here's the forecast for the next 7 days:
  Tuesday: 71°F and sunny
  Wednesday: 72°F and sunny
  Thursday: 73°F and partly cloudy
  Friday: 71°F and rainy
  Saturday: 77°F and sunny
  Sunday: 76°F and sunny
  Monday: 74°F and sunny

sections.heading: Define a Function
sections.body:
You can create and manage Functions in your application from the Atlas UI or
by importing the Function configuration and source code using the App Services
CLI or GitHub deployment.

Atlas UI

sections.heading: Step 1: Authenticate a MongoDB  User
sections.body:
Use your [API Access Lists endpoint](atlas-admin-api-access) to
log in to the App Services CLI:

```shell
appservices login --api-key="<API KEY>" --private-api-key="<PRIVATE KEY>"
```

sections.heading: Step 2: Pull Your App's Latest Configuration Files
sections.body:
Run the following command to get a local copy of your configuration files:

```shell
appservices pull --remote=<App ID>
```

By default, the command pulls files into the current working directory. You can
specify a directory path with the optional `--local` flag.

sections.heading: Step 3: Write the Function Source Code
Step 4: Configure the Function
sections.body:
In the `functions` directory of your local application, open the
`config.json` file and add a [configuration object](atlas/app-services/reference/config/functions) for your new Function to the array.

The object must have the following form:

```json
{
  "name": "<Function Name>",
  "private": <Boolean>,
  "can_evaluate": { <JSON Expression> },
  "disable_arg_logs": <Boolean>,
  "run_as_system": <Boolean>,
  "run_as_user_id": "<App Services User ID>",
  "run_as_user_id_script_source": "<Function Source Code>"
}
```

- Configure the user authentication:

  Functions in Atlas always execute in the context of a specific
  application user or as a system user that bypasses rules. To configure the
  Function's execution user, specify the type of authentication that
  Atlas should use.

  > **Note:** Functions for Database and Scheduled Triggers always execute
  > in the context of a system user.

  - **System User**: To execute the Function as a system user, use
    the following configuration:

    ```json
    {
       "run_as_system": true,
       "run_as_user_id": "",
       "run_as_user_id_script_source": ""
    }
    ```

  - **Script**: To execute the Function as a user returned from another Function, use the following configuration:

    ```json
    {
       "run_as_system": false,
       "run_as_user_id": "",
       "run_as_user_id_script_source": "<Function Source Code>"
    }
    ```

  - **User (Deprecated)**: To execute a Function as a specific
    user, use the following configuration:

    ```json
    {
       "run_as_system": false,
       "run_as_user_id": "<App Services User Id>",
       "run_as_user_id_script_source": ""
    }
    ```

- Configure the Function logs:

  To include the arguments that the Function received in the log
  entry for each execution of the Function, set `disable_arg_logs`
  to `false`.

- Specify an Authorization expression:

  You can dynamically authorize requests based on the contents of
  each request by defining a **Can Evaluate**
  [expression variables](atlas/app-services/rules/expressions). Atlas evaluates the
  expression whenever the Function is called. If you do not specify
  an expression, Atlas automatically authorizes all
  authenticated incoming requests.

  The expression can expand standard [expression variables](atlas/app-services/rules/expressions), including the [%%request](atlas/app-services/rules/expressions) and
  [%%user](atlas/app-services/rules/expressions) expansions.

  For example, the following expression only authorizes incoming
  requests if the sender's IP address is not included in the
  specified list of addresses:

  ```json
  {
     "%%request.remoteIPAddress": {
        "$nin": [
           "248.88.57.58",
           "19.241.23.116",
           "147.64.232.1"
        ]
     }
  }
  ```

- Configure the privacy level:

  To prevent client applications from seeing or calling the
  Function, set `private` to `true`.

codeBlocks.content:
{
  "name": "<Function Name>",
  "private": <Boolean>,
  "can_evaluate": { <JSON Expression> },
  "disable_arg_logs": <Boolean>,
  "run_as_system": <Boolean>,
  "run_as_user_id": "<App Services User ID>",
  "run_as_user_id_script_source": "<Function Source Code>"
}

sections.heading: Step 5: Deploy Your Changes:
sections.body:
Push the Function configuration and source code to deploy it to your app. You can begin using the Function immediately.

Run the following command to [deploy](atlas/app-services/apps/update) your changes:

```shell
appservices push
```

sections.heading: Define a Function
sections.body: You can define a new server-side Function from the Atlas UI:

sections.heading: Step 1: Navigate to the  Page
sections.body:
- Click the **Linked App Service: Triggers** link.

- In the sidebar, click **Functions** under the **Build** heading.

- Click **Create a Function**. The **Settings** tab displays by default.

sections.heading: Step 2: Name the New Function
sections.body:
Enter a name for the Function in the **Name** field. This name
must be unique from all other Functions in the application.

> **Tip:** You can define Functions inside of nested folders. Function names
> are slash-separated paths, so a Function named `utils/add` maps
> to `functions/utils/add.js` in the app's configuration files.

sections.heading: Step 3: Configure User Authentication
sections.body:
Functions in Atlas always execute in the context of a specific
application user or as a system user that bypasses rules. To configure the
Function's execution user, specify the type of authentication that
Atlas should use.

> **Note:** Functions for Database and Scheduled Triggers always execute in the
> context of a system user.

| Authentication Type                     | Description                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| --------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Application Authentication (Deprecated) | Deprecated. This type of authentication configures a Function to run in the context of the existing application user that was logged in when the client application called the Function. If the Function was called from another Function then it inherits the execution user from that Function. |
| System                                  | This type of authentication configures a Function to run as a system user that has full access to MongoDB CRUD and Aggregation APIs and is not affected by any rules, roles, or permissions.                                                                                                      |
| User ID (Deprecated)                    | Deprecated. This type of authentication configures a Function to always run as a specific application user.                                                                                                                                                                                       |
| Script                                  | This type of authentication configures a Function to run as a specific application user determined based on the result of a custom Function that you define. The Function must return a specific user's id string or can specify a system user by returning \{ "runAsSystem": true }.             |

sections.heading: Step 4: Configure Function Logs
sections.body:
By default, Atlas includes the arguments that a Function received in
the log entry for each execution of the Function.

To prevent Atlas from logging the arguments,
disable **Log Function Arguments**.

sections.heading: Step 5: Specify an Authorization Expression
sections.body:
You can dynamically authorize requests based on the contents of each request
by defining a **Can Evaluate** [expression variables](atlas/app-services/rules/expressions).
Atlas evaluates the expression whenever the Function is called. If
you do not specify an expression, Atlas automatically
authorizes all authenticated incoming requests.

The expression can expand standard [expression variables](atlas/app-services/rules/expressions),
including the [%%request](atlas/app-services/rules/expressions) and [%%user](atlas/app-services/rules/expressions)
expansions.

![The Function's Can Evaluate JSON expression input in the UI](/atlas-ui/images/triggers/function-can-evaluate-realm-ui.png)

sections.heading: Step 6: Configure the Function's Privacy Level
sections.body:
By default, you can call a Function from client applications as well
as other Functions in the same application. You can prevent client
applications from seeing or calling a Function by setting
**Private** to `true`.

You can still call a private Function from [expression variables](atlas/app-services/rules/expressions) and
other Functions, including incoming webhooks and Triggers.

![The private Function toggle in the UI](/atlas-ui/images/triggers/private-function-toggle.png)

sections.heading: Step 7: Write the Function Code
Step 8: Save the Function
sections.body:
Click **Save**. After you save the Function, you can begin using it immediately.

App Services CLI

sections.heading: Call a Function
Call from a Function
Call from the App Services CLI
sections.body:
You can call a Function through the App Services CLI with the
[function run](atlas/app-services/cli/appservices-function-run) command. The command returns the
Function result as EJSON as well as any log or error messages.

```sh
appservices function run \
  --name=sum \
  --args=1 --args=2
```

By default, Functions run in the [system functions](atlas-system-functions). To call a Function in the context of a specific
user, include their User ID in the `--user` argument.

```sh
appservices function run \
  --name=sum \
  --args=1 --args=2 \
  --user=61a50d82532cbd0de95c7c89
```

sections.heading: Constraints
sections.body:
- Functions are capped at 300 seconds of runtime
  per request, after which a Function will time out and fail.

- Functions may use up to 350MB of memory at any time.

- Functions are limited to 1000 async operations.

- Functions support most commonly used ES6+ features and Node.js
  built-in modules. However, some features that are uncommon or unsuited
  to serverless workloads are not supported. For more information, see
  [JavaScript Support](atlas-js-support).

- A Function may open a maximum of 25 sockets using the [net](https://nodejs.org/docs/v10.18.1/api/net.html) built-in module.

- Incoming requests are limited to a maximum size of 18 MB. This limit
  applies to the total size of all arguments passed to the Function.
